subject: Chemistry
chapter: Classification of Elements and Periodicity in Properties
options: Both Statement I and Statement II are true
Both Statement I and Statement II are false
Statement I is true but Statement II is false
Statement I is false but Statement II is true
solution:
Statement I:
The third ionization energy ($IE_3$) corresponds to the removal of the third valence electron. Let us look at the electronic configurations of the divalent cations for the given metals:
$Sc^{2+}$: $[Ar] 3d^{1}$
$Mn^{2+}$: $[Ar] 3d^{5}$
$Cu^{2+}$: $[Ar] 3d^{9}$
$Zn^{2+}$: $[Ar] 3d^{10}$

For $Sc$, removing the third electron involves taking it from the $3d^{1}$ state, which results in a highly stable noble gas core ($[Ar]$). Hence, $Sc$ has the lowest $IE_3$.
For $Zn$, the third electron must be removed from a completely filled, highly stable $3d^{10}$ subshell. Combined with its high effective nuclear charge ($Z_{eff}$) across the 3d series, $Zn$ requires the highest energy for the removal of the third electron.
Thus, Statement I is true.

Statement II:
The Crystal Field Stabilization Energy (CFSE) depends on the magnitude of the crystal field splitting energy ($\Delta_o$), which in turn depends on the oxidation state of the central metal ion and the strength of the ligand.
1. Oxidation state: A higher oxidation state on the central metal ion leads to a larger $\Delta_o$. Therefore, the splitting for $Co^{3+}$ complexes is significantly greater than for $Co^{2+}$ complexes. This makes the CFSE of $[Co(H_2O)_6]^{3+}$ greater than that of $[Co(H_2O)_6]^{2+}$.
2. Nature of the ligand: Ethylenediamine ($en$) is a strong field bidentate ligand, whereas $H_2O$ is a weak field ligand. According to the spectrochemical series, $en$ causes a much larger splitting than $H_2O$. Thus, the $\Delta_o$ and CFSE for $[Co(en)_3]^{3+}$ are greater than those for $[Co(H_2O)_6]^{3+}$.

The overall order of CFSE is:
$[Co(H_2O)_6]^{2+} < [Co(H_2O)_6]^{3+} < [Co(en)_3]^{3+}$
Thus, Statement II is true.

Since both statements are correct, the correct option is (1).

Answer: Both Statement I and Statement II are true

subject: Chemistry
chapter: Classification of Elements and Periodicity in Properties
solution:
The expected order of melting points of group 13 elements is B>Al>Ga>In>Tl.

But due to structural changes, melting point instead of decreasing, increases from Ga to Tl and Ga has the lowest melting point. Hence, the actual order is B>Al>Tl>In>Ga

Melting point

Al→933 K

Ga→303 K

In→430 K

Se→490 K

Ga has low meting point and it is closer to metalloid in periodic table.

subject: Chemistry
chapter: Classification of Elements and Periodicity in Properties
solution:
(A) 3 s2→Mg

(B) 3 s23p1→Al

(C) 3 s23p3→P

(D) 3 s23p4→S

Al has one unpaired electron in its highest energy orbital 3p, and Mg's highest energy orbital 3s has the paired electrons.

It is energetically favourable for all the electrons in an orbital to be paired, which means that breaking up this pair would require more energy.

Compared to 16th group, 15 group have high ionisation energy. It is because of the stable half filled configuration of he 15th group elements.

P-3s23p3

S-3s23p4

Therefore, P has more ionisation energy than S.

Hence, the correct order of first ionization enthalpy is B<A<D<C

subject: Chemistry
chapter: Classification of Elements and Periodicity in Properties
options: Both (A) and (R) are true and (R) is the correct explanation of (A)
Both (A) and (R) are true but (R) is not the correct explanation of (A)
solution:
Metal Sc Ti V Cr Mn Fe Co Ni Cu Zn
IE (kJ/mol) 631 656 650 653 717 762 758 736 745 906
 
Metal Be Mg Ca Sr Ba Ra
IE (kJ/mol) 899 737 590 549 503 509

In the first transition series, the first and second ionization enthalpy values increase slowly on moving across the period. As the nuclear charge rises, the interaction between the nucleus and valence electrons increases, and as a result, the ionization enthalpy increases. So, Sc has the lowest value of ionization enthalpy while Zn has the highest value of ionization enthalpy.

So, both assertion and reasons are true and reason is the correct expalantion of assertion.

subject: Chemistry
chapter: Classification of Elements and Periodicity in Properties
solution:
In a period, as we move from left to right, the atomic size decreases and the effective nuclear charge increases.

Due to the large atomic size and low effective nuclear charge, the element at the extreme left (alkali metal) has the lowest first ionisation enthalpy in the period.

As we move to the extreme right (excluding noble gases), the elements (halogens) have the smallest atomic size and highest effective nuclear charge in the period. They require only one electron to complete their octet. Thus, they have the highest negative electron gain enthalpy.

Therefore, the first ionisation enthalpy of the extreme left element is the lowest, and the negative electron gain enthalpy of the extreme right element is the highest.

Answer: lowest and highest

subject: Chemistry
chapter: Classification of Elements and Periodicity in Properties
solution:
A. F > P > S > B (IE₁): F has highest IE₁, P > S due to half-filled $3p^3$ stability, B has lowest. Correct
B. Cl > F > S > P (EA): Cl has highest EA (F smaller, more repulsion), S > P (half-filled p-orbital). Correct
C. K > Al > Mg > B (Metallic character): Incorrect. Mg (Group 2) is more metallic than Al (Group 13) in same period. Correct order: K > Mg > Al > B
D. $K_2O > Na_2O > MgO > Al_2O_3$ (Basic character): Follows electropositive nature. $Al_2O_3$ is amphoteric. Correct
Correct statements: A, B and D only.

subject: Chemistry

solution:
Rb & Cs have nearly same electron gain enthalpy Their values are nearly =-46 kJ/mol

Ar & Kr also have same ΔHe.g.. Value is +96 kJ/mol.

Na and K have different values (-53 and -48 kJ/mol) . And I and At has values of -295 and -270 kJ/mol. 

As we move down the group value of electron gain enthalpy decreases, with few exceptions.

Al3+,Mg2+ and Na+ are isoelectronic ionic species. For monatomic ionic isoelectronic species as positive charge increases ionic size decreases.

The order of size of Na+& K+ is Na+<K+,

∴ Increasing order of ionic radii:  Al3+<Mg2+<Na+<K+

subject: Chemistry
chapter: Classification of Elements and Periodicity in Properties
options: Both Statement I and Statement II are correct.
Both Statement I and Statement II are incorrect.
Statement I is correct but Statement II is incorrect.
Statement I is incorrect but Statement II is correct.
solution:
In Cl2 molecule, the covalent radius is half of the internuclear distance, so statement I is false. The covalent radius is little smaller than the actual radius of the atom of chlorine due to overlapping during formation of covalent bond.

For the same element, anion has lower effective nuclear charge than atom so, anion is larger than the parent atom. That means statement II is correct.

subject: Chemistry
chapter: Classification of Elements and Periodicity in Properties
solution:
Second ionization potential (I₂) is the energy to remove the second electron.
After removing the first electron: C⁺ has 2p¹, N⁺ has 2p², O⁺ has 2p³ (half-filled, stable), F⁺ has 2p⁴.
C has lowest I₂ as 2p electrons are least tightly bound with lowest nuclear charge.
N has slightly higher I₂ than C due to increased nuclear charge.
F has highest I₂ due to highest effective nuclear charge.
O⁺ with half-filled 2p is relatively stable, making I₂ of O intermediate.
Order: $C < N < F < O$

subject: Chemistry
chapter: Classification of Elements and Periodicity in Properties
solution: In the third period, Al comes after Mg and before Si. Since in Mg (1s2 2s2 2p6 3s2 ), the last electron is to be lost from a fully filled 3s-orbital while in Al (ls2 2s2 2p6 3s2 3p1 ) it is to be lost from a 3p-orbital, therefore, ∆iH1 of Al is lower than that of Mg (737 kJ mol-1). Further, nuclear charge increases in going from Na to Al, therefore ∆iH1 of Al is higher than that of Na(496 kJ mol-1) but lower than that of Mg (737 kJ mol-1). Therefore, the appropriate value of ionisation potential of  Al is 577 kJ mol-1.

subject: Chemistry
chapter: Classification of Elements and Periodicity in Properties
solution:
Ionization enthalpy is the energy required to remove an electron from an isolated gaseous atom or ion. It is always an endothermic process, so its value is always positive.

The second ionization enthalpy is the energy required to remove an electron from a unipositive ion. Since the effective nuclear charge increases after the removal of the first electron, the remaining electrons are held more tightly by the nucleus. Thus, the second ionization enthalpy is always greater than the first ionization enthalpy ($IE_{2} > IE_{1}$).

Given the first ionization enthalpy of Mg is $+737$ kJ/mol, the second ionization enthalpy must be positive and greater than $+737$ kJ/mol.

Among the given options, only $+1450$ kJ/mol satisfies these conditions.

Answer: $+1450$ kJ/mol
